---
title: "Duality"
description: "Dual spaces, dual bases, the double dual, dual maps, annihilators, and the matrix transpose as the matrix of the dual map — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §3.F."
date: "2026-06-06"
date-modified: "2026-06-23"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Section 3.F of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

## Dual space
Given some vector space $V$ over $\mb{F}$, the space of all linear maps from $V$ to $\mb{F}$---i.e. $\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})$---is called the **dual space** $V'$ of $V$. A linear map in $\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})$ is called a **linear functional**.

<details class="example">
<summary>Linear functional on $\mb{F}^n$</summary>

The function $\phi:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}$ defined as

$$\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=c_1x_1+\cdots+c_nx_n$$

where $c_1,\cdots,c_n\in\mb{F}$ is a linear functional on $\mb{F}^n$.

</details>

:::{#thm-}
Since $\dim\mb{F}=1$, each linear functional is either surjective or zero.
:::

Note that, for a finite-dimensional $V$ and any non-zero $v\in V$, we can construct a linear functional that sends $v$ to $1$. 

#### Dual of product space
:::{#thm-dual-of-product}
$(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n)'$ is isomorphic to $V'_1\times\cdots\times V'_n$.
:::

:::{.informal}
The dual of the product space is isomorphic to the product space of the duals.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
This is just a special case of a previous result.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Previously, we showed that $\mathcal{L}(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n,W)$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(V_1,W)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V_n,W)$. The result follows if we take $W=\mb{F}$.

</details>

#### Dimension of dual space
:::{#thm-dim-dual}
The dimension of $V'$ is equal to that of $V$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Recall the expression for the dimension of $\mathcal{L}(V,W)$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Before, we saw that $\dim\mathcal{L}(V,W)=(\dim V)(\dim W)$. Since $\dim\mb{F}=1$, it follows that 

$$\dim V'=\dim\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})=\dim V.$$

</details>

#### Evaluation map
Given some vectors $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ in a finite-dimensional $V$, the corresponding **evaluation map** $\Gamma:V'\to\mb{F}^n$ takes a linear functional and produces its evaluations at each of these vectors:

$$\Gamma(\phi)=(\phi(v_1),\cdots,\phi(v_n)).$$

:::{#thm-evaluation-injective}
The evaluation map is injective if and only if $\text{span}(v_1,\cdots,v_n)=V$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If the span does not cover the entire space, two functionals may "look the same" in terms of the evaluation, while disagreeing outside the span.

If the span covers the entire space, then a functional that vanishes at the evaluated vectors must vanish everywhere. 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $\Gamma$ is injective. This means that every linear functional in $V'$ sends at least one $v_i$ differently from the others. In other words, the values at $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ specifies $\phi$ uniquely.

Let $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ be a basis of $\text{span}(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$. For contradiction, suppose that $\text{span}(v_1,\cdots,v_n)\neq V$. In that case, we can extend it with $w'_1,\cdots,w'_{p'}$ to form a basis of $V$.

If so, two linear functionals can agree on the $w_i$s, and thereby on the $v_i$s, but be different maps because they disagree on the $w'_i$s. This contradicts the assertion that $\Gamma$ is injective. 

Therefore, if $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ does not span $V$, then $\Gamma$ is not injective.

Now suppose that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ does span $V$. Let us prune them such that $v_1,\cdots,v_m$ is a basis of $V$ (possibly relabelling the vectors). If we have

$$\phi(v_1)=\cdots=\phi(v_n)=0,$$ 

then for any arbitrary

$$v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_mv_m\in V,$$

we have

$$\phi(v)=a_1\phi(v_1)+\cdots+a_m\phi(v_m)=0.$$

We find that $\phi=0$, which implies that $\ker\Gamma=\{0\}$. So $\Gamma$ is injective.

Hence, $\Gamma$ is injective if and only if $\text{span}(v_1,\cdots,v_n)=V$.

</details>

:::{#thm-evaluation-surjective}
Furthermore, the evaluation map is surjective if and only if the evaluated vectors are linearly independent.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
As we saw before, a linearly independent set of vectors can be mapped to anywhere, while there are restrictions on the image of a linearly dependent set.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ are linearly independent. Then we can extend it with $v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ to form a basis of $V$.

This linear independence means that, for any $(x_1,\cdots,x_n)\in\mb{F}^n$, a linear functional $\phi\in V'$ can be defined such that $\phi(v_i)=x_i$ (while $\phi(v'_i)$ can be arbitrarily set). So $\Gamma$ is surjective.

Conversely, suppose that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ are linearly dependent. Then one of its vectors is in the span of the others, so without loss of generality, suppose that

$$v_1=a_2v_2+\cdots+a_nv_n.$$

But this implies that

$$\phi(v_1)=a_2\phi(v_2)+\cdots+a_n\phi(v_n),$$

so every sequence in the image of $\Gamma$ is constrained to the form

$$(a_2\phi(v_2)+\cdots+a_n\phi(v_n),\phi(v_2),\cdots,\phi(v_n)).$$

Then $\Gamma$ is not surjective, because

$$(a_2\phi(v_2)+\cdots+a_n\phi(v_n)+\alpha,\phi(v_2),\cdots,\phi(v_n))\notin\im\ \Gamma$$

for any $\alpha\neq0$.

Therefore, $\Gamma$ is surjective if and only if $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ are linearly independent.

</details>

#### Dual basis
Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ be a basis of $V$. The **dual basis** corresponding to this basis is the set $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n\in\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})$ of linear functionals where each $\phi_i$ is defined through

$$
\phi_i(v_j)=
\begin{cases}
1&\text{if }i=j\\
0&\text{otherwise}
\end{cases}=\delta_{i,j}.
$$

<details class="example">
<summary>Dual basis of standard basis</summary>

Consider the linear functionals $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}$ where $\phi_i$ extracts the $i$th coordinate: 

$$\phi_i(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=x_i.$$ 

For the standard basis $e_1,\cdots,e_n$, we see that $\phi_i(e_j)=1$ if $i=j$, and $\phi_i(e_j)=0$ otherwise. Therefore, $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ is the dual basis of $e_1,\cdots,e_n$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Dual basis of $(\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R}))'$</summary>

Consider the basis $1,x,\cdots,x^n$ of $\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R})$. Its dual basis is given by $\phi_0,\cdots,\phi_n:\mathcal{P}_n(\mathbb{R})\to\mb{F}$ where

$$\phi_i(p)=\frac{p^{(i)}(0)}{i!}.$$

For the basis $1,x-5,\cdots,(x-5)^n$ (note that it is a linearly independent set of the right size), the corresponding dual basis is

$$\phi_i(p)=\frac{p^{(i)}(5)}{i!}.$$

</details>

:::{#thm-dual-basis-is-basis}
As the name suggests, a dual basis is indeed a basis of $V'$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
It suffices to show that a dual basis is linearly independent, as it already has the right size.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Since $\dim V'=n$, we only have to show the linear independence of the functionals. Suppose that

$$a_1\phi_1+\cdots+a_n\phi_n=0.$$

This means that

$$(a_1\phi_1+\cdots+a_n\phi_n)(v_i)=0$$

holds for each $i=1,\cdots,n$, which implies $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$. So they are linearly independent, and the $\phi_i$s form a basis of $V'$.

</details>

###### Coordinates of linear functional in dual basis
:::{#thm-functional-coordinates-dual}
With respect to the dual basis of $v_1,\cdots,v_n$, the $i$th coordinate of a linear functional $\psi$ is given by $\psi(v_i)$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ be the dual basis of $v_1,\cdots,v_n$. Then for arbitrary functional $\psi\in V'$, we have

$$\psi=a_1\phi_1+\cdots+a_n\phi_n\implies\psi(v_i)=a_i$$

as a consequence of $\phi_i(v_j)=\delta_{i,j}$. Therefore, $\psi$ can be expressed as

$$\psi=\psi(v_1)\phi_1+\cdots+\psi(v_n)\phi_n.$$

</details>

###### Tuple of linearly independent functionals
:::{#thm-independent-functionals-surjective}
Let $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ be linearly independent functionals in $V'$. Then the map $\Phi:V\to\mb{F}^n$ defined as

$$\Phi(v)=(\phi_1(v),\cdots,\phi_n(v))$$

is surjective.
:::

:::{.informal}
Linearly independent functionals $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ are so "unrelated" to each other such that, by going over all $v\in V$, we can obtain every possible combination of $n$ "numbers".
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If $\Phi$ is not surjective, there is a non-trivial linear map $\psi:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}$ that has $\im\ \Phi$ as its kernel. But then this $\psi$ is a linear constraint between $\phi_1(v),\cdots,\phi_n(v)$, which contradicts the linear independence of the $\phi_i$s.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

For contradiction, suppose that $\im\ \Phi\subset\mb{F}^n$. Let $w_1,\cdots,w_k$ be a basis of $\im\ \Phi$, and extend it with $w'_1,\cdots,w'_{n-k}$ to form a basis of $\mb{F}^n$. Let $\psi_1,\cdots,\psi_k,\psi'_1,\cdots,\psi'_{n-k}:\mb{F}^n\to\mb{F}$ be the dual to this basis. Then we have $\psi'_1(w)=0$ for all $w\in\im\ \Phi$. 

Now, let $e_1,\cdots,e_n$ be the standard basis of $\mb{F}^n$, and let $e'_1,\cdots,e'_n$ be the corresponding dual basis. Then we can write 

$$\Phi(v)=(\phi_1(v),\cdots,\phi_n(v))=\phi_1(v)e_1+\cdots+\phi_n(v)e_n,$$

and the functional $\psi'_1$ can be expressed as

$$\psi'_1=\psi'_1(e_1)e'_1+\cdots+\psi'_1(e_n)e'_n.$$

Here, the coordinates $\psi'_1(e_i)$s are not all zero, because otherwise $\psi'_1$ would be a zero functional. So for arbitrary $v\in V$, we find

$$
\begin{aligned}
0&=\psi'_1(\Phi(v))\\
&=\psi'_1(\phi_1(v)e_1)+\cdots+\psi'_1(\phi_n(v)e_n)\\
&=\psi'_1(e_1)\phi_1(v)+\cdots+\psi'_1(e_n)\phi_n(v).
\end{aligned}
$$

But this implies that $\psi'_1(e_1)\phi_1+\cdots+\psi'_1(e_n)\phi_n$ is the zero functional, although the $\psi'_1(e_i)$s not all zero. This contradicts the assertion that the $\phi_i$s are linearly independent. Therefore, $\im\ \Phi=\mb{F}^n$.

</details>

###### Basis corresponding to arbitrary dual basis
:::{#thm-basis-for-dual-basis}
Let $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ be a basis of $V'$. Then there always exist basis $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ of $V$ whose dual basis is $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$.
:::

:::{.informal}
Any basis of $V'$ is dual to some basis of $V$, like how any basis of $V$ has a dual basis in $V'$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Since $(\phi_1(v),\cdots,\phi_n(v))$ ranges over the entire $\mb{F}^n$, we can find $v_i$s such that $\phi_j(v_i)=\delta_{i,j}$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Since $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ are linearly independent, we know by the previous result that there exist $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ such that $\phi_i(v_j)=\delta_{i,j}$. We now need to show that these form a basis of $V$.

Since $\dim V=\dim V'$, this set has the right size to be a basis. If we have

$$0=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n,$$

then acting on both sides by $\phi_i$ shows $a_i=0$. So the $v_i$s are linearly independent and form a basis of $V$. 

</details>

#### Double dual space
:::{#thm-double-dual}
The **double dual space** $V''$ of $V$ is the dual space of $V'$. If $V$ is finite-dimensional, then $\Lambda:V\to V''$ defined as

$$(\Lambda v)(\phi)=\phi(v)$$

for all $v\in V$ and $\phi\in V'$ is an isomorphism between $V$ and $V''$.
:::

:::{.informal}
The dual space of $V'$ is not the same space as $V$---after all, it consists of linear maps, not vectors. But if $V$ is finite-dimensional, then they are isomorphic, where the isomorphism sends $v$ to "functionals should evaluate me at $v$". 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Since we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\Lambda (v+w))(\phi)&=\phi(v+w)\\
&=\phi(v)+\phi(w)\\
&=(\Lambda v)(\phi)+(\Lambda w)(\phi)\\
&=(\Lambda v+\Lambda w)(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

by the linearity of $\phi$, we see that $\Lambda$ is a linear map.

If $\Lambda v=0$, then $\phi(v)=0$ for all $\phi\in V'$. This is only possible if $v=0$, and so $\Lambda$ is injective.

To show that $\Lambda$ is surjective, first note that we have 

$$\dim V=\dim V'=\dim V''.$$ 

Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ be a basis of $V$. Let $v'_1,\cdots,v'_n$ be the basis of $V'$ dual to the $v_i$s, and let $v''_1,\cdots,v''_n$ be the basis of $V''$ dual to the $v'_i$s.

An arbitrary functional $\phi'\in V''$ can be expressed as

$$\phi'=\phi'(v'_1)v''_1+\cdots+\phi'(v'_n)v''_n.$$

For an arbitrary functional $a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_nv'_n\in V'$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi'(a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_nv'_n)&=(\phi'(v'_1)v''_1+\cdots+\phi'(v'_n)v''_n)(a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_nv'_n)\\
&=a_1\phi'(v'_1)+\cdots+a_n\phi'(v'_n).
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, let $v=\phi'(v'_1)v_1+\cdots+\phi'(v'_n)v_n$. Then we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\Lambda v)(a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_nv'_n)&=(a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_nv'_n)(\phi'(v'_1)v_1+\cdots+\phi'(v'_n)v_n)\\
&=a_1\phi'(v'_1)+\cdots+a_n\phi'(v'_n)\\
&=\phi'(a_1v'_1+\cdots+a_nv'_n).
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, we have $\phi'=\Lambda v$, which implies that $\Lambda$ is surjective.

Therefore, $\Lambda$ is an isomorphism.

</details>

## Dual map
Not only do spaces and bases have their duals, but so do linear maps. But this is where it becomes slightly confusing, because the dual space is already construed as consisting of linear maps. As it were, the dual map is a linear map *between linear maps* of vectors.

For a linear map $T:V\to W$, its **dual map** $T':W'\to V'$ sends each functional $\phi\in W'$ to

$$T'(\phi)=\phi\circ T.$$

So from a map $\phi$ of vectors in $W$, we obtain a map $T'(\phi)$ of vectors in $V$ by first sending them to vectors in $W$ through $T$. Why would we want this convoluted concept? Again, patience is needed.

:::{#thm-}
The following properties are trivial to show, where $S$ and $T$ are appropriate linear maps.

- $(S+T)'=S'+T'$
- $(\lambda T)'=\lambda T'$
- $(ST)'=T'S'$
:::

<details class="example">
<summary>Dual map of identity map</summary>

The dual map $I'\in\mathcal{L}(V')$ of the identity map $I$ on $V$ acts as

$$I'(\phi)=\phi\circ I$$

for $\phi\in V'$. Then for an arbitrary $v\in V$, we have

$$I'(\phi)(v)=\phi(Iv)=\phi(v),$$

which means that $I'(\phi)=\phi$ and $I'$ is the identity map on $V'$.

<\details>

#### Two canonical isomorphisms
###### Isomorphism of subspace dual
:::{#thm-subspace-dual-iso}
Let $U$ be a subspace of $V$, and let $i:U\to V$ be the inclusion map (in which case $i':V'\to U'$). If $V$ is finite-dimensional, then $\tilde{i'}$ is an isomorphism between $V'/U^0$ and $U'$.
:::

:::{.informal}
The dual space of $U$ is isomorphic to the dual space of $V$ quotiented by the annihilator of $U\subseteq V$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

By the first isomorphism theorem, we know that $\tilde{i'}$ is an isomorphism between $V'/\ker i'$ and $\im\ i'$. So we only need to prove that $\ker i'=U^0$ and $\im\ i'=U'$.

As for the first requirement, we have

$\phi\in\ker i'$.

$\iff$ $0=(i'(\phi))(u)=(\phi\circ i)(u)=\phi(i(u))=\phi(u)$ for all $u\in U$.

$\iff$ $\phi\in U^0$.

As for the second requirement, we have $\im\ i'\subseteq U'$ by definition. Let $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ be a basis of $U'$, and suppose its extension with $\phi'_1,\cdots,\phi'_m$ gives us a basis of $V'$.

Let $\phi=a_1\phi_1+\cdots+a_n\phi_n$ be an arbitrary functional on $U'$. It is also a functional on $V'$, taking the remaining coordinates to be zero. Then for arbitrary $u\in U$, we have

$$
(i'(\phi))(u)=(\phi\circ i)(u)=\phi(u),
$$

where $\phi$ is taken to be a linear functional on $V$. Therefore, $\phi=i'(\phi)$ and so $\phi\in\im\ i'$. This implies $U'\subseteq\im\ i'$ and hence $U'=\im\ i'$.

</details>

###### Isomorphism of annihilator
:::{#thm-annihilator-iso}
Let $U$ be a subspace of $V$, and let $\pi:V\to V/U$ be a quotient map. Then $\pi':(V/U)'\to V'$ is an isomorphism between $(V/U)'$ and $U^0$. 
:::

:::{.informal}
The annihilator of $U$ is isomorphic to the dual space of $V/U$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Again, the first isomorphism theorem shows that $\pi'$ is an isomorphism between $(V/U)'/\ker\pi'$ and $\im\ \pi'$. So it suffices to show that $\ker\pi'=\{0\}$ and $\im\ \pi'=U^0$.

If $\pi'(\phi_1)=\pi'(\phi_2)$ for some $\phi_1,\phi_2\in(V/U)'$, then we have for any $v\in V$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\pi'(\phi_1))(v)=(\pi'(\phi_2))(v)\\
\implies&\phi_1(\pi(v))=\phi_2(\pi(v))\\
\implies&\phi_1(v+U)=\phi_2(v+U).
\end{aligned}
$$

But this just implies that $\phi_1=\phi_2$, and so $\pi'$ is injective, which means that $\ker\pi'=\{0\}$.

Suppose that $\phi\in V'$ is also in $\im\ \pi'$. Let $\psi\in(V/U)'$ be the functional such that $\pi'(\psi)=\phi$. Then for any $u\in U$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(u)&=(\pi'(\psi))(u)\\
&=(\psi\circ\pi)(u)\\
&=\psi(u+U)\\
&=0
\end{aligned}
$$

as $u+U$ is the additive identity of $V/U$. So we have $\im\ \pi'\subseteq U^0$.

Suppose that $\phi\in U^0$. We have $\phi\in\im\ \pi'$ if there exists $\psi\in(V/U)'$ such that 

$$\phi=\pi'(\psi)=\psi\circ\pi.$$

But we have found earlier that such $\psi$ exists when $U\subseteq\ker\phi$. As $\phi$ is an annihilator of $U$, this requirement holds, and so $U^0\subseteq \im\ \pi'$. Therefore, we have $U^0=\im\ \pi'$.

</details>

## Annihilator
Now let's move on to something that seems rather unrelated to dual maps. For a subspace $U\subseteq V$, its **annihilator** $U^0\subseteq V'$ is the set of linear functionals that sends all $u\in U$ to zero:

$$U^0=\{\phi\in V':\phi(u)=0\text{ for all }u\in U\}.$$

Alternatively, the annihilator can be described as a set of $\phi\in V'$ such that $U\subseteq\ker\phi$.

:::{#thm-}
An annihilator is a subspace of $V'$.
:::

#### Annihilator of subspace sum
:::{#thm-annihilator-sum}
If $U$ and $W$ are subspaces of $V$, then $(U+W)^0=U^0\cap W^0$.
:::

:::{.informal}
The annihilator of the sum is the intersection of the annihilators, which makes sense. To annihilate all $u+w$s, we must annihilate all $u$s and $w$s separately.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If $\phi$ annihilates $U$ and $W$, then it must annihilate $U+W$. If it doesn't, then it must have a "blind spot".
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If $\phi\in U^0\cap W^0$, then we have $\phi\in U^0$ and $\phi\in W^0$. So

$$\phi(u+w)=\phi(u)+\phi(w)=0+0=0$$

for all $u\in U$ and $w\in W$, which means $\phi\in(U+W)^0$.

Instead suppose that $\phi\notin U^0\cap W^0$, and without loss of generality, suppose that $\phi\notin U^0$. Then there exists $u\in U$ such that $\phi(u)\neq 0$. 

So while $u+0\in U+W$, we have $\phi(u+0)\neq0$. Therefore, $\phi\notin(U+W)^0$.

This implies that $(U+W)^0=U^0\cap W^0$.

</details>

#### Dimension of annihilator
:::{#thm-dim-annihilator}
If $U$ is a subspace of a finite-dimensional $V$, then

$$\dim U + \dim U^0=\dim V.$$

:::

:::{.informal}
Recall that, for a subspace $U$ of finite-dimensional $V$, we have the equality

$$\dim U + \dim V/U=\dim V.$$

Now we see that $\dim U^0=\dim V/U$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
An annihilator of $U$ becomes a zero map when restricted to $U$, so $U^0$ can be viewed as a kernel.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

$U^0$ is the kernel of the restriction map $r:\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})\to\mathcal{L}(U,\mb{F})$ defined as $r(\phi)=\phi|_U$. Since $r$ is surjective, the rank-nullity theorem gives us

$$\dim U^0=\dim\ker r=\dim\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})-\dim\mathcal{L}(U,\mb{F})=\dim V-\dim U$$

as required.

</details>

:::{#thm-}
A corollary of this is that $U$ is trivial if and only if $U^0=V'$, while $U^0$ is trivial if and only if $U=V$.
:::

#### Condition for one annihilator to contain another
:::{#thm-annihilator-containment}
Suppose that $V$ is finite-dimensional, while $U$ and $W$ are subspaces of $V$. Then $U\subseteq W$ if and only if $W^0\subseteq U^0$.
:::

:::{.informal}
A subspace contains another subspace if and only if the annihilator of the latter contains that of the former.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If $U$ is contained in $W$, it is possible to create a functional that annihilates $U$ but not $W$.  
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $U\subseteq W$. Then any annihilator of $W$ will annihilate $U$, and so $W^0\subseteq U^0$.

Suppose that $W^0\subseteq U^0$. Let $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ be a basis of $W$. For contradiction, suppose there exists $u\in U-W$. Then the set $w_1,\cdots,w_n,u$ is linearly independent, and we can extend it to a basis of $U$.

Subsequently, we can define $\phi:V\to\mb{F}$ such that it sends all the $w_i$s to zero but not $u$. This annihilates $W$ but not $U$, which is a contradiction. Therefore, all vectors in $U$ are contained in $W$.

</details>

#### What becomes annihilated by annihilator
:::{#thm-double-annihilator-subspace}
Suppose $V$ is finite-dimensional and $U$ is a subspace of $V$. Then $U$ can be characterised as

$$U=\{v\in V:\phi(v)=0\text{ for all }\phi\in U^0\},$$

i.e. the set of vectors that becomes annihilated when acted upon by any annihilator of $U$.
:::

:::{.informal}
It is trivially true that $\phi(u)=0$ for all $u\in U$, so this really amounts to saying that no other vectors are annihilated by $U^0$ except the vectors in $U$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Given the dimension of the annihilator, the dimension of the annihilated is fixed.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $u_1,\cdots,u_n$ be a basis for $U$. Suppose there exists $w\notin U$ such that $\phi(w)=0$ for all $\phi\in U^0$. 

Let $W$ be the subspace spanned by $u_1,\cdots,u_n,w$. Then we have $\dim W^0=\dim U^0$, while $\dim W=\dim U+1$. Since

$$\dim V=\dim U+\dim U^0=\dim W+\dim W^0,$$

we have a contradiction, and so no such $w$ exists.

</details>

#### Annihilator of annihilated
:::{#thm-double-annihilator-functionals}
Suppose $V$ is finite-dimensional and $\Gamma$ is a subspace of $V'$. Then $\Gamma$ can be characterised as

$$\Gamma=\{v\in V:\phi(v)=0\text{ for all }\phi\in \Gamma\}^0.$$

:::

:::{.informal}
Suppose we have a subspace of functionals, and find the vectors that become annihilated by these functionals. Then no functionals outside this subspace annihilates these vectors.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Show that the two subspaces have the same dimension, by considering a map that evaluates each vector at the basis functionals of $\Gamma$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

For brevity, let us denote the set on the right as $U^0$. Tautologically, a functional $\phi\in\Gamma$ will annihilate the vectors annihilated by $\phi\in\Gamma$, and so $\Gamma\subseteq U^0$.

We will show that $\dim U^0=\dim\Gamma$, thereby completing the proof. Let $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ be a basis of $\Gamma$ (where $n=\dim\Gamma$), and define a map $M:V\to\mb{F}^n$ as

$$Mv=(\phi_1(v),\cdots,\phi_n(v)).$$

(This looks like an evaluation map, except it is now the vectors that are being evaluated by multiple functionals.)

If $v\in\ker M$, then $\phi_1(v)=\cdots=\phi_n(v)=0$ and so $\phi(v)=0$ for all $\phi\in\Gamma$. Therefore, $\ker M\subseteq U$.

If $v\in U$, then $\phi(v)=0$ for all $\phi\in\Gamma$, which includes the functionals $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$. Therefore, $U\subseteq \ker M$.

So we have $U=\ker M$. Furthermore, since $\phi_1,\cdots\phi_n$ are linearly independent, $M$ is surjective by a previous result. Then the rank-nullity theorem gives us

$$\dim V=\dim\ker M+\dim\im\ M=\dim U+\dim \Gamma.$$

Since $\dim V=\dim U^0+\dim U$, this implies $\dim U^0=\dim\Gamma$ as required.

</details>

#### Some equalities
Now we have 4 equalities that we can prove in succession, although only the third one is memorable.

###### Kernel of dual map is annihilator of image of vector map
:::{#thm-kernel-dual-map}
That is, $\ker T'=(\im\ T)^0$ where $T:V\to W$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

A linear functional $\phi\in W'$ is in $\ker T'$ if and only if 

$$0=T'(\phi)=\phi\circ T.$$

This means that we have $0=\phi(Tv)$ for all $Tv\in\im\ T$. So $\phi\in\ker T'$ if and only if $\phi\in(\im\ T)^0$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>When kernel of dual map is span of linear functional</summary>

Suppose that $\phi$ is a linear functional on $\mathcal{P}_5(\mathbb{R})$ defined as $\phi(p)=p(8)$. Let $T\in\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{P}_5(\mathbb{R}),\mathcal{P}_5(\mathbb{R}))$ be a map whose dual map $T'$ satisfies $\ker T'=\text{span}(\phi)$. We wish to find $\im\ T$.

Then by the above result, we have $(\im\ T)^0=\text{span}(\phi)$. By a previous result, no other vectors are annihilated by $\text{span}(\phi)$ except those in $\im\ T$. Therefore, a vector is in $\im\ T$ if and only if it lies in the kernel of $\phi$. So we have

$$\im\ T=\{p\in\mathcal{P}_5(\mathbb{R}):p(8)=0\}.$$

</details>

###### Dimension of kernel of dual map in terms of three other dimensions
:::{#thm-dim-kernel-dual}
If $V$ and $W$ are finite-dimensional, then

$$\dim\ker T'=\dim\ker T+\dim W-\dim V.$$

:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

By the previous two results, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim\ker T'&=\dim(\im\ T)^0\\
&=\dim W-\dim\im\ T.
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\dim\im\ T=\dim V-\dim\ker T$ by the rank-nullity theorem, the desired equality follows.

</details>

###### Images of vector map and dual map have same dimension
:::{#thm-rank-dual-equal}
That is, $\dim\im\ T'=\dim\im\ T$ where $V$ and $W$ are finite-dimensional.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The rank-nullity theorem and the previous result gives us

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim\im\ T'&=\dim W'-\dim\ker T'\\
&=\dim W-(\dim\ker T+\dim W-\dim V)\\
&=\dim V-\dim\ker T,
\end{aligned}
$$

where we also used $\dim W=\dim W'$ in the second line. Then the desired equality follows from another use of the rank-nullity theorem.

</details>

A corollary of this is that $T'=0$ if and only if $T=0$.

###### Image of dual map is annihilator of vector map
:::{#thm-image-dual-map}
That is, $\im\ T'=(\ker T)^0$, which is a "mirror image" of what we proved earlier.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The proof here is a bit different from the others. First, let $\phi\in V'$ be a functional in $\im\ T'$. Then there exists $\psi\in W'$ such that $T'(\psi)=\phi$. For any $v\in\ker T$, we have

$$\phi(v)=(T'(\psi))(v)=\psi(Tv)=\psi(0)=0,$$

and so $\phi$ is an annihilator of $\ker T$. So $\im\ T'\subseteq(\ker T)^0$.

Now we use the fact that a subspace and its enclosing space are actually equal if they have the same dimension. By the previous result and the rank-nullity theorem, we have 

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim\im\ T'&=\dim\im\ T\\
&=\dim V-\dim\ker T.
\end{aligned}
$$

But we saw previously that $\dim V-\dim\ker T=\dim(\ker T)^0$, so we find that $\im\ T'$ and $(\ker T)^0$ have the same dimension, as required.

</details>

#### Injectivity and surjectivity
:::{#thm-dual-injective-surjective}
$T$ is surjective (resp. injective) if and only if $T'$ is injective (resp. surjective).
:::

:::{.informal}
Perhaps intuitively for something called a "dual map", the injection of one is equivalent to the surjection of the other.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Both proofs involve the previous results at appropriate places. 

$T$ is surjective and $\im\ T=W$.

$\iff$ $\dim (\im\ T)^0=0$ and $(\im\ T)^0=\{0\}$.

$\iff$ $\ker T'=\{0\}$

$\iff$ $T'$ is injective.

$T$ is injective.

$\iff$ $\dim(\ker T)=0$.

$\iff$ $\dim(\ker T)^0=\dim V'$ and $(\ker T)^0=V'$.

$\iff$ $\im\ T'=V'$ and $T'$ is surjective.

</details>

#### Dimension of intersection of kernels
:::{#thm-dim-intersection-kernels}
If $V$ is finite-dimensional and $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n\in V'$ are linearly independent, then

$$\dim(\ker\phi_1\cap\cdots\cap\ker\phi_n)=\dim V-n.$$

:::

:::{.informal}
Each linearly independent linear functional leaves a "different dimension" of the vector space alive.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
The intersection of the kernels of $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ is annihilated by any $\phi_i$, and it turns out that the annihilator of this intersection is spanned by $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

First, note that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dim(\ker\phi_1\cap\cdots\cap\ker\phi_n)&=\dim V-\dim((\ker\phi_1\cap\cdots\cap\ker\phi_n)^0).
\end{aligned}
$$

We conjecture that $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ form a basis of $(\ker\phi_1\cap\cdots\cap\ker\phi_n)^0$. 

Firstly, we see that the $\phi_i$s do lie inside this annihilator, because they annihilate any vector in the intersection of their kernels.

The $\phi_i$s are linearly independent by the problem statement, so we only have to check that they span $(\bigcap_{i=1}^n\ker\phi_i)^0$.

By an equality shown previously, we have

$$(\bigcap_{i=1}^n\ker\phi_i)^0=(\ker\phi_1)^0+\cdots+(\ker\phi_n)^0.$$

Now, we can express $\ker\phi_i$ as

$$\ker\phi_i=\{v\in V:\phi(v)=0\text{ for all }\phi\in\Phi_i\},$$

where $\Phi_i=\{\lambda\phi_i:\lambda\in\mb{F}\}$. Then another result above allows us to obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\ker\phi_i)^0&=\{v\in V:\phi(v)=0\text{ for all }\phi\in\Phi_i\}^0\\
&=\Phi_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

So we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\bigcap_{i=1}^n\ker\phi_i)^0&=\Phi_1+\cdots+\Phi_n\\
&=\{\lambda\phi_1:\lambda\in\mb{F}\}+\cdots+\{\lambda\phi_n:\lambda\in\mb{F}\}\\
&=\{\lambda_1\phi_1+\cdots+\lambda_n\phi_n:\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mb{F}\},
\end{aligned}
$$

which means that $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ do span $(\bigcap_{i=1}^n\ker\phi_i)^0$.

Therefore, $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ forms a basis of $(\ker\phi_1\cap\cdots\cap\ker\phi_n)^0$, which means that its dimension is $n$ as required.

</details>

## Matrix transpose
Given a $m\times n$ matrix $A$, its **transpose** $A^T$ is the $n\times m$ obtained by interchanging the row and column indices of $A$, i.e.

$$(A^T)_{i,j}=A_{j,i}.$$

:::{#thm-}
The map ${}^T:\mb{F}^{m,n}\to\mb{F}^{n,m}$ is an isomorphism.
:::

#### Transpose of product
:::{#thm-transpose-product}
The transpose of matrix product is the matrix product of transposes. That is, if $A$ and $B$ are $m\times n$ and $n\times p$ matrices respectively, then $(AB)^T=B^TA^T$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

For any index of $(AB)^T$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
((AB)^T)_{i,j}&=(AB)_{j,i}\\
&=\sum_{k=1}^n A_{j,k}B_{k,i}\\
&=\sum_{k=1}^n (B^T)_{i,k}(A^T)_{k,j}\\
&=(B^TA^T)_{i,j},
\end{aligned}
$$

which implies $(AB)^T=B^TA^T$ as a whole. 

</details>

#### Matrix of dual map
Let $V$ and $W$ be finite-dimensional with dimensions $n$ and $m$ respectively. Let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ be a basis of $V$, and let $\phi_1,\cdots,\phi_n$ be the corresponding dual basis in $V'$. Let $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ be a basis of $W$, and let $\psi_1\cdots,\psi_m$ be the corresponding dual basis in $W'$. Let $\mathcal{M}(T)$ and $\mathcal{M}(T')$ be the $m\times n$ and $n\times m$ matrix representations of $T:V\to W$ and $T':W'\to V'$ in these bases respectively.

:::{#thm-matrix-dual-transpose}
Then the matrix of the dual map is the transpose of the matrix of the original map, i.e.

$$\mathcal{M}(T')=\mathcal{M}(T)^T.$$

:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Consider the definition of $\mathcal{M}(T')$ and "reveal" each entry by using the basis of $V$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

By the definition of matrix representation, we have

$$T'(\psi_j)=\sum_{i=1}^n\mathcal{M}(T')_{i,j}\phi_i.$$

Let us have both sides act on a basis vector $v_k$. On the RHS, we find

$$\sum_{i=1}^n\mathcal{M}(T')_{i,j}\phi_i(v_k)=\sum_{i=1}^n\mathcal{M}(T')_{i,j}\delta_{i,k}=\mathcal{M}(T')_{k,j}.$$

On the LHS, we find

$$
\begin{aligned}
T'(\psi_j)(v_k)&=\psi_j(Tv_k)\\
&=\psi_j(\sum_{i=1}^m\mathcal{M}(T)_{i,k}w_i)\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^m\mathcal{M}(T)_{i,k}\delta_{j,i}\\
&=\mathcal{M}(T)_{j,k}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, we see that $\mathcal{M}(T')=\mathcal{M}(T)^T$.

</details>

## Rank
#### Row and column ranks
Let $A$ be an $m\times n$ matrix. Its **row rank** is the dimension of

$$\text{span}(A_{1,\cdot},\cdots,A_{m,\cdot}),$$

which is a subspace of $\mb{F}^{1,n}$. Its **column rank** is the dimension of

$$\text{span}(A_{\cdot,1},\cdots,A_{\cdot,n}),$$

which is a subspace of $\mb{F}^{m,1}$.

###### Column rank is dimension of image
:::{#thm-column-rank-image}
The column rank of $\mathcal{M}(T)$ is the same as $\dim\im\ T$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $T:V\to W$, and consider its matrix representation $\mathcal{M}(T)$. By the definition of $\mathcal{M}(T)$, its $i$th column is the matrix representation of $Tv_i$ in the chosen basis of $W$. 

So the column rank of $\mathcal{M}(T)$ is equal to the dimension of $\text{span}(\mathcal{M}(Tv_1),\cdots,\mathcal{M}(Tv_n))$ where $n=\dim V$.

But we have an isomorphism between $\text{span}(\mathcal{M}(Tv_1),\cdots,\mathcal{M}(Tv_n))$ and $\text{span}(Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n)$ in the form of

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
a_1\\
\vdots\\
a_m
\end{pmatrix}
\mapsto a_1w_1+\cdots+a_mw_m,
$$

where $m=\dim W$. (Note that the domain is a subspace of $\mb{F}^{m,1}$, while the image is a subspace of $W$.)

So we have the equality

$$\dim\text{span}(\mathcal{M}(Tv_1),\cdots,\mathcal{M}(Tv_n))=\dim\text{span}(Tv_1,\cdots,Tv_n)=\dim\im\ T.$$

Therefore, the column rank is equal to $\dim\im\ T$.

</details>

###### Column and row ranks are equal
:::{#thm-column-row-rank-equal}
It turns out that the column rank and row rank of a matrix are always the same, and so we can unambiguously speak of its **rank**.
:::

:::{.informal}
This is why we referred to $\dim\im\ T$ as the rank of $T$ earlier.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Any $m\times n$ matrix $A$ can be interpreted as the matrix representation of some $T:V\to W$, where $V$ and $W$ have dimensions $n$ and $m$ respectively. Then we have by the previous result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{column rank of }A&=\dim\im\ T\\
&=\dim\im\ T'\\
&=\text{column rank of }A^T\\
&=\text{row rank of }A,
\end{aligned}
$$

where we used the third of the 4 equalities in the second line. 

</details>

###### Matrix of rank one
:::{#thm-}
A matrix has rank one if and only if there exist $c_1,\cdots,c_m$ and $d_1,\cdots,d_n$ such that $A_{i,j}=c_id_j$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The matrix $A$ has rank one if and only if its row rank is one. The row rank is equal to one if and only if each row is a scalar multiple of a common row. If we designate this common row by $(d_1,\cdots,d_n)$, and the scalar factors of the rows as $c_1,\cdots,c_m$, then the matrix satisfies

$$A_{i,\cdot}=c_id\implies A_{i,j}=c_id_j.$$

(Subsequently, we find that each column is a scalar multiple of the common column $(c_1,\cdots,c_m)$, with the scalar factors given by $(d_1,\cdots,d_n)$. As expected, the column rank is also one.)

</details>

###### Matrix of operator
:::{#thm-operator-matrix-invertible}
Let $T$ be an operator of finite-dimensional $V$, and let $\mathcal{M}(T)$ be its matrix representation with respect to two bases of $V$. Then $T$ is invertible if and only if the columns or the rows of $\mathcal{M}(T)$ either are linearly independent or span $\mb{F}^{n,1}$ or $\mb{F}^{1,m}$ respectively.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

$T$ is invertible.

$\iff$ $T$ is surjective.

$\iff$ $\dim\im\ T=n$.

$\iff$ The column (row) rank of $\mathcal{M}(T)$ is $n$.

$\iff$ The dimension of the span of the columns (rows) is $n$.

$\iff$ The columns (resp. rows) form a basis of $\mb{F}^{n,1}$ (resp. $\mb{F}^{1,m}$).

$\iff$ Being of the right size, the columns (resp. rows) either are linearly independent or span $\mb{F}^{n,1}$ (resp. $\mb{F}^{1,m}$).

</details>
